steps.narrationText: First we will set a secret in env mode. The value will land as an environment variable inside the container. Notice the empty current flag, we are declaring this is a brand new secret with no prior value.

Now a file mode secret. File mode never puts the value in the container environment. It writes it to a file at slash run slash secrets, using Docker standard mechanism. Prefer this for anything sensitive.
Let us list what we have. Names and modes only. The list never shows values, no matter who is asking.
Now the part that surprises people. The secret get command returns a digest, not the value. There is no flag that gives the plaintext back. That is the GitHub Actions model: write only by design.
Lost the value? Do not peek. Rotate. The rotate-secret flag skips the precondition check, and the audit log marks the change as a deliberate rotation. Loud, intentional, safe.
Now the punchline. We will fork the repo. The fork is a byte-for-byte copy of the parent encrypted image.
But when we list the fork secrets: empty. No Stripe key, no database password, no API token. The fork cannot pretend to be you. That is why clone production, break nothing actually works.

narrationAudio.narrationText: Time to put real credentials in a place forks cannot reach! We will set two secrets, prove they are write-only, then fork the repo and watch them disappear.

Most teams drop a dot env file into the repo because it is the obvious move. The problem is the fork inherits a byte-for-byte copy of the image, so your sandbox boots with the production Stripe key and starts charging real customer cards.
The CLI follows the GitHub Actions model: get returns a digest, never the value. If you lose track of a secret, you do not peek, you rotate, and the audit log records it as a deliberate change.
Here is the payoff. The fork shares the parent's encrypted data, but the secret store is scoped per repo, so the fork lists empty and cannot interpolate any of your production keys.
And that is secrets locked down where forks cannot follow. See you in the next one!
